current_fact.fact_text: The Titanic story took a new turn on 18 April 1912, when survivors stepped off the Carpathia into New York City and into a storm of cameras, questions, and grief. Their arrival turned a distant maritime disaster into a shared urban trauma, etched into global memory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #50 - Postcards from Earth-London
episode.description:
Postcards from Earth-London is a Morning Drops walk through the city Gaia has called home for the past ten years, a place made of sound, memory, and late-night streets. 

Four tracks guide the journey: from iconic landmarks to quieter corners, from romance to reality checks, and back to the reason the city still feels alive: music.

current_fact.match_reason: Survivors arriving in a great harbor city after an ocean crossing resonate with an urban sound-walk through another world city, both turning streets into carriers of memory.

queue.fact_text: The British military experiments that produced the Doolittle Raid and Operation Vengeance hinged on codebreaking and radio, but they also hinted at a future of remote, technology-mediated power. These April 18 operations foreshadowed our era, where invisible signals can redirect planes, economies, and attention across the globe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

queue.match_reason: Invisible wartime signals that once steered planes resonate with an episode about invisible digital streams steering our ears, inviting more conscious, intentional use of such power.

queue.fact_text: Across different centuries, 18 April has seen wars declared, earthquakes unleashed, protests ignited, and even a superhero born. It’s a reminder that a single calendar day can hold everything from tectonic shifts beneath our feet to quiet shifts in how we imagine freedom, identity, and power.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: A day packed with turning points across history makes a fitting companion to Sedna FM’s own origin episode—the moment a small broadcast began radiating outward into a larger story.

published.fact_text: In 1902, a magnitude 7.5 earthquake ripped through western Guatemala, shaking the ground so violently that entire towns were twisted out of shape. Between 800 and 2,000 people were killed, and the event helped seismologists refine how they describe shaking intensity on scales like the Modified Mercalli scale.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A sudden, violent jolt of the Earth pairs with an "electric shock" of guitar-driven energy, echoing raw, seismic power.

published.fact_text: On 18 April 1906, San Francisco woke before dawn to a magnitude 7.9 earthquake so powerful it snapped gas lines and ignited fires that burned for days. More than 3,000 people died, and the disaster reshaped not only the city’s skyline but also modern urban planning and earthquake science.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A city leveled by shaking and fire finds resonance in an episode about weathering storms and rebuilding through change and hope.

published.fact_text: In 1912, the RMS Carpathia steamed through iceberg-filled North Atlantic waters to rescue 705 survivors of the Titanic. For hours, its crew hauled shivering passengers from lifeboats, turning an ordinary liner into an unlikely symbol of human solidarity at sea.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A night of lifeboats and cold ocean is paired with an episode about immersion and water-like flow, transforming a sea of danger into one of reflection.

published.fact_text: On 18 April 1915, pioneering French aviator Roland Garros was shot down over World War I battle lines and forced to glide his damaged aircraft to a crash-landing behind German lines. He had just tested one of the earliest systems for firing a machine gun through a propeller, a risky innovation that changed aerial combat forever.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: Garros’s boundary-breaking aviation mirrors trip-hop’s boundary-blurring sound, both experimenting with new technologies to rewrite what’s possible in the air and in the studio.

published.fact_text: In 1797, Napoleon Bonaparte and Austrian envoy Maximilian, Count of Merveldt, signed the Peace of Leoben, an armistice that effectively ended the War of the First Coalition. The deal was hammered out so quickly that it outpaced official diplomats, showcasing Napoleon’s audacious habit of reshaping Europe with a pen as much as with cannons.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

published.match_reason: A fiery young general reshaping Europe pairs with an episode about Italian contrasts—fog and volcanic energy—echoing the mix of cold diplomacy and explosive ambition.

published.fact_text: On Good Friday in 1930, a wooden church in the Romanian town of Costești caught fire during crowded services, trapping worshippers inside. The blaze spread so fast through the dry timber that 118 people—many of them schoolchildren—died in minutes, prompting a national reckoning over safety in traditional wooden churches.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: A tragic fire during a sacred gathering is paired with an introspective episode about bodily vulnerability and slow healing, offering a space to process loss and fragility.

published.fact_text: In 1775, British troops rowed quietly up the Charles River in Massachusetts, aiming to seize colonial military supplies. Riders like Paul Revere fanned out ahead of them through the night, turning a covert operation into the spark that ignited the American Revolution.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: The night ride that helped launch a revolution connects with an episode steeped in protest music, amplifying themes of resistance and defiance against power.

published.fact_text: On 18 April 1897, Greece and the Ottoman Empire plunged into the Greco-Turkish War over the fate of Crete, an island that had become a flashpoint of national ambitions and ethnic tensions. The conflict was brief but charged with symbolism, feeding the idea of a modern Greek nation while exposing its military weaknesses.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

published.match_reason: A war over a Mediterranean island is matched with a musical voyage across the same sea, blending Greek and Turkish sounds where politics once drew hard borders.

published.fact_text: In 1902, the North Sea island of Heligoland shook under "Operation Big Bang," the largest non-nuclear man-made explosion at the time. The British detonated around 6,700 tonnes of surplus munitions to erase German bunkers, blasting a new cratered landscape into existence in a single thunderous moment.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: An island-shattering detonation matches an episode built as a long crescendo, rising from quiet textures to explosive, danceable energy.

published.fact_text: On 18 April 1942, sixteen U.S. bombers led by James Doolittle lifted off from an aircraft carrier in the Pacific in a mission many thought impossible. Their surprise raid on Tokyo, Yokohama, Kobe and Nagoya caused limited physical damage but delivered a massive psychological shock, proving Japan’s home islands were not untouchable.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

published.match_reason: A daring mission that reclaims psychological space is paired with an episode about people reclaiming physical and cultural space on the dancefloor—both are about crossing thresholds others said you couldn’t.

published.fact_text: Also in 1942, Pierre Laval—one of the most controversial figures in French politics—returned as Prime Minister of Vichy France, the collaborationist regime that administered unoccupied France under Nazi oversight. His tenure deepened France’s complicity in deportations and repression, leaving scars that still haunt the country’s memory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A regime defined by unhealthy dependence on occupying powers is mirrored by an episode dissecting our own uneasy dependencies—here on technology—inviting critical reflection on the choices we normalize.

published.fact_text: On 18 April 1943, U.S. codebreakers and fighter pilots executed "Operation Vengeance," intercepting and shooting down the plane of Admiral Isoroku Yamamoto, the architect of Pearl Harbor. The mission relied on decrypted Japanese communications and precise navigation over Bougainville Island, showcasing how invisible information could decisively shape a very physical battlefield.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A mission built on codebreaking and meticulous planning pairs with an episode celebrating the power of learning and invisible mental work to transform real-world outcomes.

published.fact_text: On 18 April 1946, Jackie Robinson took the field for the Montreal Royals, quietly making them the first integrated modern professional baseball team. Before he broke Major League Baseball’s color barrier, he faced hostile crowds and teammates in the minors, turning every stolen base into an act of social courage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

published.match_reason: Robinson’s barrier-breaking presence on the field pairs with an episode centered on hip-hop voices breaking gender barriers, both pushing into spaces they were told weren’t for them.

published.fact_text: In 1949, the Republic of Ireland Act came into force, formally declaring Éire a republic and ending its constitutional link with the British Crown. With a stroke of legal language, Ireland stepped out of the British Commonwealth and into a new, self-defined political identity.

episode.title: Morning Drops #18 - Sardegna
episode.description: Yasmin traces her connection to Sardegna — the island where her Italian journey began. Through music and memory, she shares the story of how this place shaped her family's path, and why it still holds such a deep place in her heart.
songs: Mahmood & Tenores di Bitti - Com’è profondo il mare

published.match_reason: A nation redefining its identity and relationship to a former power resonates with an episode about how a specific place shapes personal and cultural belonging.

published.fact_text: On 18 April 1955, leaders from 29 newly independent or still-colonized Asian and African nations convened in Bandung, Indonesia. It was a bold experiment in postcolonial diplomacy, where countries that had been spoken for in global politics began, for the first time, to speak together in their own collective voice.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: A conference where postcolonial nations asserted their voice pairs naturally with an episode celebrating Afrobeat, a postcolonial musical language that did the same in sound.

published.fact_text: On 18 April 1938, a caped figure in red and blue quietly appeared in Action Comics #1: Superman, one of the first modern superheroes. Printed on cheap pulp paper, his debut helped invent an entire genre where ordinary people could imagine extraordinary alter-egos.

episode.title: Sedna FM #06 - Alters of an Ego
episode.description: What if you could step outside yourself and become someone freer, bolder, or completely unexpected? Exploring the artistry of musicians who craft alter-egos and parallel realities to experiment with new dimensions of their identity. It's not about hiding who they are — it's about expanding who they can be.
songs: The Fireman - Strawberries Oceans Ships Forest

published.match_reason: Superman’s secret identity and super-ego align perfectly with an episode about musical alter-egos and the art of becoming someone more than your everyday self.

published.fact_text: On 18 April 1972, East African Airways Flight 720 overran the runway during a rejected takeoff at Addis Ababa’s Bole International Airport. The accident killed 43 people and became a stark case study in how split-second cockpit decisions and runway conditions can tip a routine departure into disaster.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A journey that never truly takes off is coupled with an episode about travel and movement, reframing motion as something that can also happen in the mind when physical travel goes wrong.

published.fact_text: On 18 April 1996, Israeli artillery shelled a UN compound near the Lebanese village of Qana, where hundreds of civilians had taken shelter during fighting. The Qana massacre killed 106 people and wounded over a hundred more, turning a supposed safe haven into a symbol of the human cost of modern warfare.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: A brutal strike on civilians and a UN shelter is paired with an artist known for sharp political critique and human stories from conflict-marked regions, offering voice where there was silencing.

published.fact_text: On 18 April 2018, protests erupted across Nicaragua after the government announced unpopular social security reforms. Within days, the demonstrations swelled into a broader movement against President Daniel Ortega’s rule, revealing deep frustrations in a country long presented as stable.

episode.title: Morning Drops #13 - Demystifying Reggaeton
episode.description: Yasmin takes us on a journey through the real story of reggaeton — from its roots in Panama and Puerto Rico to its global evolution. Exploring the genre's history, influences, and cultural impact.
songs: Renato - Chica de los Ojos Café
B. F. Yaviah - Si Tu Quieres Fumar

published.match_reason: Latin American street protests and youth-led dissent are echoed by an episode digging into reggaeton’s roots—another Latin urban force born from marginalized voices and resistance.

published.fact_text: Also on 18 April 2018, King Mswati III announced that Swaziland would change its official name to Eswatini, "land of the Swazis" in the local language. The move marked 50 years of independence and was a symbolic attempt to shed colonial naming in favor of indigenous identity.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: A country reclaiming its indigenous name aligns with an episode honoring African musical movements that reclaimed sound and language from colonial shadows.

published.fact_text: On 18 April 1902, Guatemala’s earthquake wasn’t the only natural drama on Earth; on the small North Sea island of Heligoland, decades later, humans would reshape rock with explosives. Together these stories reveal how both tectonic forces and human decisions can redraw coastlines in sudden, irreversible ways.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: Reflecting on how violently Earth can move pairs with an episode inviting us to imagine a "slow planet," offering a sonic antidote to seismic upheaval.

published.fact_text: The Titanic story took a new turn on 18 April 1912, when survivors stepped off the Carpathia into New York City and into a storm of cameras, questions, and grief. Their arrival turned a distant maritime disaster into a shared urban trauma, etched into global memory.
